section.title: A Prayer for Restoration
section.sectionRationale: The book ends not with a dirge but with a prayer. The fifth poem drops the acrostic form, as if grief has finally become direct address, and the whole community turns as one to plead with God. They lay out their disgrace, confess their sin, and rest everything on the one unshaken fact left to them: You, O LORD, reign forever. It closes with the book's boldest request and its most honest fear, holding both together before the throne.
section.thesis: Stripped of everything, the community turns from lament to direct prayer, spreading their disgrace before the eternal King, confessing that they have sinned, and casting themselves on the God who reigns forever with the plea, Restore us to Yourself, O LORD, so we may return.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the community spreads its disgrace before God, 5:1 to 18; the anchor of hope, You, O LORD, reign forever, 5:19; and the closing plea and honest fear, Restore us to Yourself, unless You have utterly rejected us, 5:20 to 22.

units.label: Remember, O LORD, our disgrace
units.note: the community spreads out its suffering

verses.text: {{zakar|Remember}}, O LORD, what has happened to us. Look and see our disgrace!
Our {{nachalah|inheritance}} has been turned over to strangers, our houses to foreigners.
We have become fatherless orphans; our mothers are widows.
We must buy the water we drink; our wood comes at a price.
We are closely pursued; we are weary and find no rest.
We submitted to Egypt and Assyria to get enough bread.
Our fathers sinned and are no more, but we bear their punishment.
Slaves rule over us; there is no one to deliver us from their hands.
We get our bread at the risk of our lives because of the sword in the wilderness.
Our skin is as hot as an oven with fever from our hunger.
Women have been ravished in Zion, virgins in the cities of Judah.
Princes have been hung up by their hands; elders receive no respect.
Young men toil at millstones; boys stagger under loads of wood.
The elders have left the city gate; the young men have stopped their music.
Joy has left our hearts; our dancing has turned to mourning.
The crown has fallen from our head. Woe to us, for we have sinned!
Because of this, our hearts are faint; because of these, our eyes grow dim,
because of Mount Zion, which lies desolate, patrolled by foxes.
You, O LORD, reign forever; Your throne endures from generation to generation.

units.label: Restore us to Yourself
units.note: the closing plea and honest fear

verses.text: Why have You forgotten us forever? Why have You forsaken us for so long?
{{shuv|Restore}} us to Yourself, O LORD, so we may return; renew our days as of old,
unless You have utterly rejected us and remain angry with us beyond measure.

zakar.gloss: To remember, and in Scripture to remember is to act, not merely to recall. When Israel asks the LORD to remember, they are pleading with Him to step in and do something about their plight, as He remembered His covenant and rescued them from Egypt.

nachalah.gloss: Inheritance, the land the LORD gave each family as their permanent portion, a sign of belonging in the promised land. To have the inheritance handed to strangers is to lose the tangible pledge of the covenant, which makes the loss far deeper than mere property.

shuv.gloss: To turn, return, restore. The same root carries both halves of the plea: restore us and we will return. It is the word for repentance, turning back to God, and here the people confess that even their turning must be God's gift, restore us that we may return.

basic.context: The last chapter is a prayer, and everyone prays it together. Notice the words us and we all the way through: this is the whole community on its knees. They stop describing the ruin from a distance and simply spread it out before God, orphans and widows, paying for their own water, ruled by slaves, joy gone, the crown fallen. They confess it plainly, woe to us, for we have sinned. Then, in one bright verse, they lay hold of the only thing that has not been shaken: You, O LORD, reign forever. On that ground they make the book's boldest request, Restore us to Yourself, so we may return, and end with an honest, trembling fear that God might have rejected them for good.
basic.hermeneutics: The dropping of the acrostic here is likely deliberate: the tidy alphabet gives way to raw, direct pleading. The center of gravity is verse 19, the one solid rock, God still reigns, His throne outlasts every empire that has fallen on them. Everything else in the prayer leans on that. The key move is in verse 21: they do not say we will pull ourselves together; they ask God to restore them so that they can return. Even repentance is understood as a gift only God can give. And the book ends unresolved, on a plea, which teaches us that faith can live in the tension of waiting.
basic.originalLanguages: The prayer opens with zakar, remember, and in the Bible when God remembers He acts; they are begging Him to intervene, as He once remembered His covenant and saved them from Egypt. Their nachalah, their inheritance, the family land that pledged their place in the covenant, is now in strangers' hands, a loss far deeper than property. And the closing plea turns on shuv, to return or restore: restore us that we may return, confessing that even their turning back to God must be His work.
basic.typology: The community that cannot restore itself and must cry restore us to Yourself pictures the human condition that only God can heal, fulfilled when God draws sinners to Himself through Christ. The eternal throne that endures forever is the throne of the LORD, established in the risen and reigning Christ. And the honest fear of being rejected forever is answered by the Son who was rejected and forsaken in our place so that we never would be.
basic.christ: The prayer restore us to Yourself, so we may return finds its answer in Jesus, through whom God turns sinners back to Himself. The eternal throne of verse 19 is the very throne now occupied by the risen Christ, whose kingdom will never end. And the book's most fearful line, that God might have utterly rejected them, is met at the cross, where Jesus is truly forsaken, why have You forsaken Me, absorbing the rejection our sin deserved so that God can say to us, I will never leave you nor forsake you.
basic.god: God is the eternal King whose throne outlasts every catastrophe: empires rise and fall, Jerusalem lies in ruins, but He reigns forever. He is the God who remembers and acts, the only one who can restore what is broken. And He is a God who welcomes brutally honest prayer, He receives both the bold request and the trembling fear, and does not turn away the ones who come pleading.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 80 repeats the plea restore us, O God, that we may be saved. Psalm 102 celebrates the LORD enthroned forever while Zion lies in ruins. Jeremiah 31 promises the new covenant and return. Matthew 27 records Jesus forsaken in our place, and Hebrews 13 promises He will never leave nor forsake His own.
basic.oneStory: The book of grief ends on its knees, resting everything on the God who reigns forever and pleading, restore us to Yourself. That prayer is the cry of every exile and every sinner, and God answers it in Christ, who is forsaken so we are welcomed home, and who reigns on the eternal throne, so that the days of God's people can truly be renewed.

context.literary: The fifth and final poem, and the only one that is not an acrostic; its twenty two lines match the alphabet's length but abandon its form, as ordered lament gives way to direct, communal prayer. Spoken throughout in the first person plural, it moves from a catalog of suffering, 5:1 to 18, to the confession of God's eternal reign, 5:19, to a closing petition and unresolved plea, 5:20 to 22.
context.historical: It reflects life in the aftermath of Jerusalem's fall, whether among the exiles or the poor left in the land: dispossession, forced labor, food insecurity, and humiliation under foreign rule. The reference to submitting to Egypt and Assyria recalls Judah's long, futile habit of seeking security from great powers instead of from the LORD.
context.canonical: The communal plea restore us echoes the refrains of the Psalms and looks ahead to the prophetic promises of return and the new covenant. Ending the book on an open ended petition, rather than a resolution, leaves God's people leaning forward in hope toward a restoration that Scripture ultimately locates in Christ and the age to come.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To gather the whole grieving community into a single prayer that honestly lays their disgrace and sin before God, anchors all hope in His eternal reign, and pleads for a restoration that only He can grant, leaving them waiting on His mercy.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The poem describes the community's suffering and humiliation; it prescribes the response of turning it all into confession and prayer. Its enduring pattern is to spread our trouble before the eternal King, to own our sin, and to ask God Himself to restore us, rather than trusting our own strength to recover.

debates.question: How should we read the troubling final verse, unless You have utterly rejected us, 5:22?

views.label: An honest fear that still hopes
views.case: The prayer ends not in despair but in a trembling question laid before God; the very act of praying verse 21 shows they do not believe rejection is final, and they leave the outcome in His hands.

views.label: A deliberate open ending
views.case: By closing on an unresolved note, the book keeps God's people leaning forward, unable to presume on restoration yet unwilling to let go of the plea; the tension is the point, and it is resolved only later in the biblical story.

debates.bearing: Either way the ending models faith that prays honestly in the dark, refusing both presumption and despair, and Scripture answers the fear elsewhere with God's promise never to forsake His own in Christ.

debates.question: How do bearing our fathers' sin, 5:7, and confessing we have sinned, 5:16, fit together?

views.label: Shared, inherited consequences of sin
views.case: The generation suffers real consequences flowing from previous generations' covenant breaking, acknowledging that sin's effects ripple down through a community over time.

views.label: Personal ownership within corporate guilt
views.case: The same prayer also says woe to us, for we have sinned, so they do not hide behind their ancestors but own their own guilt, holding inherited consequence and personal responsibility together.

debates.bearing: The prayer refuses to choose: it feels the weight of a shared history of sin and still takes personal responsibility, which is exactly the honest posture that casts itself wholly on God's mercy rather than self justification.

study.originalLanguages: The prayer is framed by two loaded words. It opens with zakar, remember: in Scripture God's remembering is never bare recollection but saving action, so to cry remember, O LORD is to plead with Him to act, as He acted when He remembered His covenant in Egypt. Their lost nachalah, the inheritance of family land, was the very pledge of their place in the covenant, so its seizure cuts far deeper than economics. And everything turns at last on shuv, to return and to restore: restore us to Yourself, O LORD, so we may return. The same root names both God's restoring and their repenting, a quiet confession that even turning back to God is a gift He must give.

typology.type: The plea restore us to Yourself, so we may return, 5:21
typology.fulfillment: God drawing sinners back to Himself through Christ, in whom repentance and restoration are His gift.

typology.type: The throne that endures from generation to generation, 5:19
typology.fulfillment: The eternal throne of the risen and reigning Christ, whose kingdom will never end, Luke 1:33.

typology.type: The fear of being forsaken forever, 5:20 and 5:22
typology.fulfillment: Christ forsaken in our place, Matthew 27:46, so that His people are never finally rejected, Hebrews 13:5.

study.christ: The book's last plea, restore us to Yourself, O LORD, so we may return, is a prayer only the gospel can fully answer, for it is God who turns sinners back to Himself through Christ. The eternal throne of verse 19, unshaken while Jerusalem lies in ruins, is the throne now occupied by the risen Jesus, whose reign no empire can topple. And the trembling fear that God may have utterly rejected them meets its answer at Calvary: there the Son cried, why have You forsaken Me, bearing the rejection our sin deserved, so that to all who are His God now says, I will never leave you nor forsake you. The unresolved ending of Lamentations is resolved in Him.
study.god: God is unveiled here as the eternal King whose throne endures from generation to generation, sovereign and steadfast while everything else the people trusted has collapsed. He is the God who remembers and therefore acts, the only one able to restore a people who cannot restore themselves. And He is a God who welcomes the rawest honesty, receiving both the bold request, renew our days as of old, and the fearful question, unless You have utterly rejected us, without turning the pleaders away. His unchanging reign is the ground on which desperate faith can still pray.

crossReferences.note: Restore us, O God; let Your face shine, that we may be saved, the same plea for restoration as a gift from God.
But You, O LORD, sit enthroned forever, echoing You, O LORD, reign forever while Zion lies in ruins.
Restore me, and I will return, for You are the LORD my God, the language of restoration and repentance as God's gift.
Jesus cries, why have You forsaken Me, bearing the forsakenness feared in Lamentations 5:20 and 22.
I will never leave you nor forsake you, God's answer to the fear that He might utterly reject His own.

study.oneStory: The book of grief ends on its knees, resting all its weight on the God who reigns forever and pleading, restore us to Yourself. That is the cry of every exile and every sinner who cannot mend what is broken. God answers it in Christ, who is forsaken so that we are welcomed home, who reigns on the unshaken throne, and in whom the days of God's people are truly renewed, so that the last word of Lamentations turns out not to be despair but hope deferred until the dawn.

quiz.q: What is distinctive about the form and voice of Lamentations 5?

options.text: It drops the acrostic form and is prayed by the whole community together in the first person plural, us and we.
options.note: Ordered lament gives way to direct, communal prayer.

options.text: It is a prayer spoken by the gathered people rather than a lone voice.
options.note: True, and it also notably abandons the acrostic structure of the earlier poems.

options.text: It is a song of triumph celebrating Zion's rebuilding.
options.note: It laments ongoing disgrace and pleads for restoration; the rebuilding has not come.

options.text: It is a list of laws for the returning exiles.
options.note: It is a communal prayer, not legal instruction.

quiz.q: What is the one unshaken anchor of the prayer, in verse 19?

options.text: That the LORD reigns forever and His throne endures from generation to generation.
options.note: God's eternal reign is the solid ground on which the whole prayer rests.

options.text: That God is still on His throne despite the ruin.
options.note: True, and specifically His throne outlasts every empire and generation.

options.text: That the walls of Jerusalem were still standing.
options.note: The city lies desolate; the anchor is God's reign, not the city's condition.

options.text: That their enemies had finally repented.
options.note: The prayer rests on God's eternal kingship, not on the enemies.

quiz.q: What does the plea Restore us to Yourself, so we may return, teach about repentance?

options.text: That even turning back to God is His gift; they ask God to restore them so that they are able to return.
options.note: The one root shuv carries both God's restoring and their returning.

options.text: That they needed God's help to come back to Him.
options.note: True, and precisely that their very repentance depends on God restoring them first.

options.text: That they could restore themselves by trying harder.
options.note: The prayer asks God to do the restoring; it does not rely on their own effort.

options.text: That returning to God was no longer possible.
options.note: They earnestly ask to return; the plea assumes it is possible by God's grace.

quiz.q: How does the community handle its guilt in this prayer?

options.text: It both feels the weight of its fathers' sin and personally confesses, woe to us, for we have sinned.
options.note: Inherited consequence and personal responsibility are held together, 5:7 and 5:16.

options.text: It owns its own sin while acknowledging a shared history of sin.
options.note: True, and it refuses to hide behind the ancestors, taking personal responsibility.

options.text: It blames the previous generations entirely and claims innocence.
options.note: It says we have sinned; it does not claim innocence.

options.text: It denies that anyone had sinned at all.
options.note: It plainly confesses sin as the cause of their woe.

quiz.q: How does the book's fearful ending, unless You have utterly rejected us, point to Christ?

options.text: The fear of being forsaken forever is answered at the cross, where Christ is forsaken in our place so His people are never finally rejected.
options.note: Jesus bears the forsakenness so God can promise never to forsake His own.

options.text: It shows a people who still hope even while fearing rejection.
options.note: True, and the gospel answers that fear in the forsaken and risen Christ.

options.text: It proves God had in fact rejected His people permanently.
options.note: The plea itself hopes otherwise, and Christ secures that they are not rejected.

options.text: It has no relation to Christ.
options.note: Christ's cry of forsakenness directly answers this fear.

questions: This whole chapter is prayed as we, not I. What difference might it make to bring your community's or family's troubles to God together, rather than always praying alone?
The people simply spread their disgrace out before God, holding nothing back. Is there something you have been too ashamed to name in prayer? What would it be like to lay it plainly before Him?
Everything in the prayer leans on verse 19, You, O LORD, reign forever. When your circumstances are shaking, how does the truth that God is still on the throne steady you?
They ask God to restore them so that they can return, treating even repentance as His gift. Where do you need to stop trying to fix yourself and ask God to do the turning in you?

The book ends on a plea, not a resolution, with the fear of being forsaken answered only later in Christ. How does it help to know you can pray honestly in the dark, trusting a God who was forsaken so that you never will be?

translationNotes.note: Remember translates zakar; in Scripture God's remembering is active, an appeal for Him to intervene, not merely to call something to mind.
Our fathers sinned and are no more, but we bear their punishment expresses the shared, inherited consequences of sin, held alongside the personal confession of 5:16.
The BSB source prints an em dash after our eyes grow dim; it is rendered here as a clean break with a comma, with no change to the wording.
Restore us so we may return uses one Hebrew root, shuv, for both God's restoring and the people's returning, quietly confessing that repentance itself is God's gift.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Lamentations 5:1-22. Verse segments concatenated in order and separated by single spaces; poetic lines rendered as flowing prose within each verse. The em dash after grow dim in 5:17 was converted to a clean break with a comma. No em or en dashes used anywhere in the file.
